title: Lemon and thyme wood furniture spray
subtitle: A diluted vinegar and olive oil spray with lemon and thyme essential oils for cleaning and conditioning wood furniture
excerpt: A small amount of olive oil in a diluted vinegar spray polishes and conditions wood furniture while the vinegar cuts through surface grime and the essential oils add a clean, herbal scent. Shake vigorously before each use as the oil separates. Suitable for unsealed and lightly finished wooden furniture; test on an inconspicuous area first. Not for use on stone, lacquered, or waxed surfaces.
sourceNotes: Wood conditioning sprays: olive oil as a wood conditioner replenishes the natural oils in wood and reduces drying. Diluted white vinegar removes surface grime and light oxidation. The combination is a traditional furniture polish base (referenced in The Complete Book of Clean, Tara Teaspoon, 2022). The ratio of oil to vinegar determines whether the spray polishes (more oil) or cleans (more vinegar). This recipe leans toward cleaning with a conditioning benefit. Essential oils: lemon EO adds fresh scent; thyme EO adds a herbal note. Not suitable for lacquered, polyurethane-finished, or waxed surfaces as vinegar can degrade sealants. Always test on an inconspicuous area.

recipe.yieldDescription: Approximately 220 ml in a spray bottle
recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe for a larger bottle. Proportions hold at double.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ready to use immediately. Shake vigorously before each use as the oil separates.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 6 months in a sealed spray bottle at room temperature. The vinegar acts as a partial preservative. Discard if the spray develops mould or an unusual smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of replenishing the natural oils in wood to prevent drying, cracking, and dulling of the surface. Oils such as olive oil penetrate unsealed wood grain and slow moisture loss. Over-application of oil can leave a greasy surface; a light mist spray followed by buffing avoids this.

content.text: in one mist: the vinegar cuts through surface grime and light oxidation; the olive oil penetrates and conditions the wood surface; lemon and thyme essential oils add a clean, herbal scent. The oil and water do not mix permanently, so shake the bottle vigorously before each spray. Apply a light mist rather than saturating; too much oil leaves a greasy surface. This spray is for unsealed or lightly finished wood surfaces. Do not use on lacquered, polyurethane-coated, or waxed surfaces as the vinegar can lift or cloud the finish. Test on a hidden area first.

items.prepNote: Conditioning agent; shakes back into suspension before use
Fresh citrus scent; mixes into the oil phase
Clean herbal note; mixes into the oil phase

content.text: In a jug, stir the essential oils into the olive oil, then add the white vinegar and distilled water. Pour into the spray bottle. Seal and label. The oil and water will separate; this is expected.

Shake the bottle vigorously for 10 seconds before each use. Spray lightly onto a clean, dry cloth (not directly onto the furniture). Wipe the wood surface with the cloth in the direction of the grain. Buff with a second dry cloth to remove any oil residue. Use on side tables, chair legs, wooden shelving, and similar furniture. Do not use on lacquered, polyurethane-coated, waxed, or painted wood. Test on an inconspicuous area before first use.
Store at room temperature. Shake before each use. The spray keeps 6 months. Discard if it develops mould or an unusual smell beyond the normal vinegar-herb character.
